title: The hidden landscape of localization
speaker: Svitlana Mayboroda
abstract: Complexity of the geometry, randomness of the potential, and many other irregularities of the system can cause powerful, albeit quite different, manifestations of localization: a phenomenon of confinement of waves, or eigenfunctions, to a small portion of the original domain.

proof_stages.label: Localization problem and need for local structure
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates localization as a problem of understanding not just global exponential decay but the visible bulk/drop structure, local density of states, and carrier overlaps in geometric, random, and semiconductor settings.

proof_stages.label: Landscape function and universal pointwise control
proof_stages.summary: For a positive elliptic operator L, the landscape u solving Lu=1 gives a universal pointwise bound on normalized eigenfunctions, so low values of u and high barriers of 1/u identify possible confinement regions.

proof_stages.label: Exponential decay in the landscape metric
proof_stages.summary: Eigenfunctions are shown to decay exponentially in a nonlinear Agmon-type distance determined by 1/u, with the proof mechanism explained through an exact conjugation identity in which 1/u becomes both an effective potential and part of the geometry.

proof_stages.label: Spectral decoupling and transport through wells
proof_stages.summary: The full spectrum and eigenfunctions are exponentially close to the combined spectra on landscape subregions, so wells are weakly coupled except at resonances and pointwise landscape distances predict transport paths.

proof_stages.label: Free-boundary definition and geometry of valleys
proof_stages.summary: Because raw level lines of the landscape are too unstable, valleys are defined through a variational free-boundary partition that recovers the landscape decomposition and yields uniform geometric properties such as nondegeneracy and interior/exterior ball access.

proof_stages.label: Spectral ordering and landscape Weyl law
proof_stages.summary: The minima and well geometry of 1/u predict the order, locations, and approximate values of low eigenfunctions, while replacing the potential by 1/u in Weyl-type phase-space counting gives accurate integrated and local density-of-states information.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of semiconductor Schrödinger-Poisson computation
proof_stages.summary: For nonlinear electron-hole Schrödinger-Poisson semiconductor models, landscape local densities of states and overlaps replace direct eigenfunction computation, greatly reducing computation time and enabling inverse design of devices.

examples.name: Rectangles versus irregular domains
examples.note: Rectangular domains with sine/cosine product eigenfunctions are contrasted with irregular domains whose modes can concentrate, making the basic notion of geometric localization concrete.
